front: Film: opening popularized Richard Strauss's 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'; 'The Blue Danube' plays during a space station docking sequence
Film: Kubrick commissioned Alex North to write a score, then rejected it entirely without telling North, using Gyorgy Ligeti and Richard Strauss instead
Film: Alex and his gang beat up writer F. Alexander and rape his wife while singing 'Singin' in the Rain'
Film: the Ludovico process cures the protagonist of 'ultraviolence' but inadvertently makes him unable to tolerate Beethoven
Film: main theme is Henry Purcell's Music for the Funeral of Queen Mary arranged for Moog synthesizer by Wendy Carlos
Film: the Korova bar is plastered with 'moloko' (Russian for milk); its furniture was inspired by Allen Jones's artworks Hatstand, Table, and Chair
Film: William Tell Overture accompanies a three-minute timelapse of the protagonist having a threesome
Film: shot entirely by candlelight using NASA Zeiss lenses (f-stop 0.7); camera slowly zooms out to mimic 18th-century painting framing
Film: the final shot recreates the last piece in Hogarth's Marriage-a-la-Mode series
Film: title character climbs the English social ladder but loses his left leg in a duel; grew out of a scrapped Napoleon biopic
Film: 'The British Grenadiers' is played on flute and snare drum during battle scenes
Film: ends with Kubrick personally airbrushing Jack Nicholson into a 1921 New Year's Eve party photo, played to 'Midnight, the Stars, and You'
Film: the twin girls were inspired by Diane Arbus's photograph Identical Twins
Film: Danny's tricycle scenes were filmed using the newly invented Steadicam (Garrett Brown)
Film: Tom Cruise infiltrates a masked orgy at a former Rothschild home by saying the password 'fidelio'
Film: orgy score uses Jocelyn Pook's reversed Romanian liturgy; originally included Sri Lankan Manickam Yogeswaran reciting the Gita (removed after protests)
Film: Cate Blanchett voiced a character's lines uncredited; a blindfolded pianist was played by director Todd Field
Film: General Jack D. Ripper orders a nuclear attack on the Soviet Union; Major Kong waves his hat while riding an atomic bomb
work: Dr. Strangelove or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb (1964)

front: Film: ends with Vera Lynn's 'We'll Meet Again' as a character worries about a 'mineshaft gap'
work: Dr. Strangelove or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb (1964)

front: Film: inspired in part by Herman Kahn, RAND Corporation analyst who wrote On Thermonuclear War
work: Dr. Strangelove or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb (1964)

front: Film: ends with a German prisoner singing 'The Faithful Hussar' to a group of soldiers; Kirk Douglas starred then reunited with the director on Spartacus
Director: started at Look magazine after selling a staged photo of a newsstand employee sad over FDR's death; first film was documentary Day of the Fight (about boxer Walter Cartier)
